title: Add Linearizability & Consistency Models interactive simulation
description: CRITICAL MISSING (P0): No coverage of consistency models — the foundational framework for understanding all distributed tradeoffs. In MIT 6.5840 LEC 8 (dedicated lecture), DDIA Ch 9 (rewritten for 2nd ed), and top-5 interview topic per MentorCruise + DesignGurus. Build interactive simulation: same set of read/write operations shown under 4 models (Linearizable, Sequential Consistency, Causal Consistency, Eventual Consistency) with visual diff of outcomes. Include a timeline showing when reads see writes under each model.
acceptanceCriteria: Engine in src/lib/distributed/consistency-models.ts supports 4 models
Visualization shows 3 nodes with read/write ops, different outcomes per model
User can toggle between models and see the SAME operations produce DIFFERENT results
Properties panel explains each model with real-world database examples (Spanner=linearizable, Cassandra=eventual)
Interview questions section included

title: Add Replication interactive simulation (Leader-Follower, Multi-Leader, Leaderless/Quorum)
description: CRITICAL MISSING (P0): No simulation for replication strategies despite having excellent written content in concepts-data.ts (slug: 'replication'). MIT 6.5840 core topic, DDIA Ch 5 entire chapter, universal interview topic. Build: (1) Leader-Follower with sync/async toggle showing replication lag, (2) Multi-Leader showing write conflicts, (3) Leaderless with adjustable NWR quorum sliders. SEO content already covers the theory — this adds the interactive experience.
acceptanceCriteria: Engine in src/lib/distributed/replication.ts supports 3 modes
Quorum mode lets user adjust N, W, R with sliders and see success/failure
Multi-leader mode shows conflict detection and resolution
Leader-follower shows replication lag visual (async) vs blocking (sync)
Properties panel links to existing SEO content for replication

title: Add Sharding / Data Partitioning interactive simulation
description: CRITICAL MISSING (P0): No simulation for sharding despite SEO content at concepts-data.ts (slug: 'sharding'). #1 most-asked distributed topic per DesignGurus, DDIA Ch 6, MIT 6.5840 Lab 5. Build: (1) Range-based sharding showing hot spot problem, (2) Hash-based sharding showing even distribution, (3) Directory-based sharding. Let user see data distribution across shards and observe rebalancing when shards are added/removed. Links naturally to existing Consistent Hashing simulation.
acceptanceCriteria: Engine in src/lib/distributed/sharding.ts supports range, hash, and directory strategies
Visualization shows data items distributed across shards with color-coding
User can add/remove shards and see redistribution
Hot spot problem visible in range-based mode
Cross-reference to Consistent Hashing simulation in properties panel

title: Add Leader Election interactive simulation (Bully, Ring, Lease-based)
description: CRITICAL MISSING (P0): Leader election is foundational to Raft/Paxos but never shown independently. MIT 6.5840, Cornell CS5414, AWS Builders' Library all cover it. Build: (1) Bully algorithm (highest ID wins), (2) Ring-based election, (3) Lease-based election (time-bounded leadership). Show how Raft's randomized timeout is a specific instance of leader election.
acceptanceCriteria: Engine in src/lib/distributed/leader-election.ts supports 3 algorithms
Visualization shows nodes communicating election messages
User can crash the leader and watch re-election
Properties panel explains connection to Raft (our existing simulation)
Each algorithm's strengths/weaknesses compared

title: Add PACELC extension to CAP Theorem simulation
description: Our SEO content (concepts-data.ts:131,139) already mentions PACELC but it's not simulated. PACELC is frequently asked in senior interviews: 'During Partition choose A or C; Else choose Latency or Consistency.' Add a PACELC toggle to the existing CAP simulation that shows Latency-Consistency tradeoff during normal (non-partitioned) operation.
acceptanceCriteria: PACELC mode toggle added to CAP controls
During non-partitioned state, shows latency vs consistency tradeoff
Properties panel explains PACELC with real system examples
Existing CAP behavior unchanged when PACELC toggle is off

title: Add orchestration mode to Saga Pattern simulation
description: Current Saga simulation only shows choreography (simulateSagaChoreography at saga.ts:46). The SEO content (concepts-data.ts:616) explains both choreography and orchestration, but only choreography is interactive. Add orchestration variant where a central coordinator directs participants, and a comparison view showing the same saga in both modes.
acceptanceCriteria: New simulateSagaOrchestration function in saga.ts
User can toggle between choreography and orchestration modes
Orchestration shows central coordinator directing each step
Comparison table in properties panel highlights tradeoffs

title: Add Gossip push/pull/push-pull variant toggle
description: Current Gossip engine (gossip.ts) only implements push gossip. The SEO content (concepts-data.ts:556) explains all three variants: push, pull, and push-pull. Add toggle to switch between variants and show convergence speed differences.
acceptanceCriteria: Gossip engine supports push, pull, and push-pull modes
User toggle in controls switches modes
Convergence bar shows speed difference between modes
Properties panel updated with variant comparison

title: Add Multi-Paxos extension to Paxos simulation
description: Current Paxos (paxos.ts) only shows single-decree Paxos. Real systems use Multi-Paxos where a stable leader drives multiple rounds without re-running Phase 1. Add Multi-Paxos mode showing: (1) Leader pre-elected, (2) Multiple values proposed in sequence, (3) Phase 1 skipped for subsequent rounds. Include comparison with Raft.
acceptanceCriteria: Multi-Paxos mode toggle added
Shows leader optimization skipping Phase 1
Multiple sequential proposals demonstrated
Comparison table with Raft in properties panel

title: Add Distributed Locking interactive simulation (Redlock, Fencing Tokens)
description: IMPORTANT MISSING (P1): SEO content exists at concepts-data.ts (slug: 'distributed-locking') with excellent 3-paragraph explanation covering Redlock, fencing tokens, and ZooKeeper. Knowledge graph has node. But no interactive simulation. Build: (1) Simple lock/unlock with TTL, (2) Redlock across 5 Redis instances, (3) Fencing token mechanism preventing stale lock holder.
acceptanceCriteria: Engine in src/lib/distributed/distributed-lock.ts
Shows lock acquisition across multiple Redis instances (Redlock)
Fencing token visualization shows how stale holders are rejected
Properties panel links to existing SEO content
Failure scenarios: clock skew, GC pause during lock hold

title: Add real-world application context to all 11 existing simulations
description: None of the 11 simulations mention where the algorithm is used in production. Add 'Real-World Usage' section to each properties panel: Raft (etcd, CockroachDB, TiKV), Consistent Hashing (Cassandra, DynamoDB, memcached), Vector Clocks (Dynamo, Riak), Gossip (Cassandra, Consul), CRDTs (Figma, Automerge, Yjs, Redis), CAP (classify 10 real databases), 2PC (XA transactions, MySQL), Saga (Temporal, AWS Step Functions), MapReduce (Hadoop, early Google), Lamport (foundational, Spanner uses TrueTime instead), Paxos (Chubby, Spanner, Megastore).
acceptanceCriteria: Each of 11 simulations has 'Used By' section in properties panel
At least 3 real production systems listed per topic
Brief explanation of HOW each system uses the algorithm
Links to further reading where available

title: Add 'When to Use' comparison guides for related topic pairs
description: Students need decision guidance: When to use Raft vs Paxos? 2PC vs Saga? Lamport vs Vector Clocks? Gossip vs Consensus? Add comparison cards to properties panels for these natural pairs. Each card: criteria table (consistency needs, # of nodes, failure tolerance, complexity) + recommendation + real-world example of each choice.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 5 comparison guides created: Raft/Paxos, 2PC/Saga, Lamport/Vector Clocks, CP/AP (in CAP), Choreography/Orchestration (in Saga)
Each comparison has a criteria table with trade-off axes
Each comparison recommends specific scenarios for each choice
Visible in properties panel when either paired topic is selected

title: Add difficulty tags and prerequisite indicators to sidebar
description: Current sidebar shows 11 items with no difficulty or ordering indication. Add: (1) Difficulty badges (Beginner: CAP, Consistent Hashing, MapReduce, Lamport / Intermediate: Vector Clocks, Gossip, 2PC, Saga / Advanced: Raft, Paxos, CRDTs), (2) Prerequisite arrows or 'Learn X first' text when a topic has dependencies (e.g., Vector Clocks shows 'Prerequisites: Lamport Timestamps').
acceptanceCriteria: Each sidebar item shows difficulty badge (color dot or tag)
Prerequisites shown as subtle text below description
Clicking a prerequisite link navigates to that simulation
Difficulty classification matches academic consensus

title: Add Distributed Vector Search (HNSW) simulation
description: EMERGING 2026 (P1): DDIA 2nd edition added vector indexes for semantic search. Every AI-era company needs this (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, pgvector). Zero competitors visualize HNSW. Build: (1) Show hierarchical navigable small world graph construction, (2) Query routing through layers, (3) Approximate nearest neighbor search with recall trade-off. This would be a first-in-the-world educational visualization.
acceptanceCriteria: Engine in src/lib/distributed/hnsw.ts models multi-layer graph
Visualization shows graph layers with search path highlighted
User can insert vectors and run queries
Recall vs speed trade-off demonstrated visually
Properties panel explains why this matters for AI applications

title: Add pseudocode panels for all 11 existing simulations
description: ZERO simulations have pseudocode despite this being the standard for algorithm education. For each: Raft (election + log replication pseudocode from paper), Consistent Hashing (findOwner binary search), Vector Clocks (merge rule), Gossip (exchange round), CRDTs (merge per type), CAP (write/read under partition), 2PC (coordinator/participant FSM), Saga (forward + compensate), MapReduce (map/shuffle/reduce), Lamport (send/receive rule), Paxos (prepare/promise/accept/learn).
acceptanceCriteria: Each of 11 simulations has a toggleable pseudocode panel
Pseudocode uses academic notation, not implementation code
Current line highlights as simulation steps through
Pseudocode is accessible (not just an image)

title: Fix MapReduce properties panel: 'embarrassingly parallel' is partially misleading
description: At DistributedModule.tsx:3313 in MapReduce properties, it says 'Embarrassingly parallel'. The Map phase IS embarrassingly parallel, but the Shuffle phase requires network communication (all-to-all exchange) and is NOT embarrassingly parallel. Clarify: 'Map phase: embarrassingly parallel. Shuffle phase: requires network all-to-all exchange. Reduce phase: parallel per key.'
acceptanceCriteria: Properties panel distinguishes parallelism of each phase
Shuffle phase's network cost mentioned explicitly
Description matches Dean/Ghemawat 2004 paper

title: Fix Consistent Hashing and Saga domain classification in knowledge graph
description: In knowledge-graph/concepts.ts, Consistent Hashing (line 356) has domain='data' and Saga (line 553) has domain='patterns'. Both are fundamentally distributed systems algorithms and should be domain='distributed' to appear correctly in the distributed systems knowledge graph view. The SEO layer correctly classifies them as category='distributed-systems'.
acceptanceCriteria: Consistent Hashing domain changed to 'distributed' in knowledge graph
Saga domain changed to 'distributed' in knowledge graph
Both still appear in their original cross-reference contexts

title: Implement 'FAANG Interview Prep' learning path for distributed systems
description: Based on 2026 interview patterns from research: Week 1 (Foundations): CAP Theorem, Consistent Hashing, Replication, Sharding. Week 2 (Protocols): Gossip, 2PC, Saga, Distributed Locking. Week 3 (Consensus): Raft, Paxos, Leader Election. Week 4 (Advanced): CRDTs, Vector Clocks, Consistency Models. Each week has: required simulations, interview questions to study, comparison guides. Track completion status.
acceptanceCriteria: 4-week learning path defined with topics per week
Each topic links to its simulation
Interview questions aggregated from SEO concept pages
Progress tracking (checkmarks for completed simulations)
Accessible from a 'Learning Paths' section in the module

title: Add common mistakes / gotchas section to top 5 simulations
description: For each of the top 5 most important topics, add a 'Common Mistakes' section based on Students' Guide to Raft and Reddit research: (1) Raft: heartbeat confusion, election timer reset timing, log matching safety. (2) CAP: thinking you 'choose 2 of 3' (you choose between C and A during partition). (3) 2PC: not understanding why it's blocking. (4) CRDTs: assuming all types handle all operations. (5) Consistent Hashing: forgetting virtual nodes importance.
acceptanceCriteria: Top 5 simulations have 'Common Mistakes' expandable section in properties
Each lists 2-3 specific mistakes with why they're wrong
References to authoritative sources (Raft paper, DDIA) where applicable

title: Add Raft log compaction/snapshotting extension
description: Current Raft simulation (raft.ts) has no log compaction. In MIT 6.5840 Lab 3D and production Raft (etcd), log compaction via snapshotting is essential. Add: (1) Snapshot trigger when log exceeds N entries, (2) Visual showing log truncation after snapshot, (3) Follower catch-up via InstallSnapshot RPC.
acceptanceCriteria: RaftCluster supports snapshot() method
Visual shows log entries being compacted into a snapshot block
InstallSnapshot RPC animated when slow follower needs catch-up
Properties panel explains why compaction is necessary

title: Add cross-module links from distributed topics to System Design module
description: Distributed systems topics connect directly to system design: Consistent Hashing → Load Balancer design, CAP Theorem → Database selection, Replication → Database design, Sharding → Database scaling, 2PC/Saga → Microservice transactions, Gossip → Service mesh. Add 'Connected System Design Topics' links in properties panels that navigate to the System Design module's relevant concepts.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 8 cross-module links defined
Each link navigates to the correct system design concept page
Bidirectional: system design module also links back to distributed simulations
Links are visually distinct from internal links
